items.q: Does secularisation mean religion has disappeared from Britain?
items.correct: No — it means religion's public influence and attendance have declined, yet Britain remains a multi-faith society where religion persists and diversifies.
items.misconception: Secularisation means nobody in Britain is religious any more.
items.random_wrong: Secularisation is a government ban on religious worship.
items.why: Students treat decline as disappearance; evaluation answers need the nuance that religion changes its social role rather than simply vanishing.

items.q: Does the Genesis idea of 'dominion' mean humans may exploit the earth freely?
items.correct: Most believers interpret dominion alongside stewardship: authority over creation carries responsibility to care for it on God's behalf, not licence to exploit it.
items.misconception: Religious teaching says humans can use the earth however they like because God gave them dominion.
items.random_wrong: Dominion is a church tax on farmland.
items.why: Students quote 'dominion' (Genesis 1:28) without the balancing stewardship teaching, producing one-sided answers on religion and the environment.

items.q: Is Zakah an optional donation in Islam?
items.correct: No — Zakah is an obligatory Pillar of Islam, normally 2.5% of qualifying wealth each year; voluntary additional charity is called sadaqah.
items.misconception: Zakah is just an optional gift Muslims make when they feel generous.
items.random_wrong: Zakah is a tax paid to the government of Saudi Arabia.
items.why: Students confuse obligatory Zakah with voluntary sadaqah; the distinction between duty and optional charity is a standard exam point.

items.correct: The declining influence of religion in society
items.misconception: The growth of new religious movements
items.random_wrong: The union of church and state
items.why: It's The declining influence of religion in society. “The growth of new religious movements” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which term describes the religious duty to care for the natural world on God's behalf?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Stewardship. “Dominion without limits” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does the sanctity of life mean?
items.correct: Life is sacred because it is given by God
items.misconception: Life should be extended at any financial cost
items.random_wrong: Only religious people's lives have value
items.why: It's Life is sacred because it is given by God. “Life should be extended at any financial cost” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Zakah in Islam and tzedakah in Judaism are both examples of what?
items.why: Correct answer: Religious duties of charitable giving. “Pilgrimage obligations” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What percentage of qualifying wealth do Muslims give annually as Zakah?
items.why: Exact answer: 2.5 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
